title: Homework 3
text: This assignment practices reading, combining, and manipulating different data types, such as Excel, comma-separated values (CSV), and databases.

title: Homework 3
text:
Glass Data
Opening this file shows that the data is delimited by commas, ,. The source of the data is here.

First we create the column names and read in the data.


glass_col_names <- c("Id", "RI", "Na", "Mg", "Al", "Si", "K", "Ca", "Ba", "Fe",
                     "Type_of_glass")

glass <- read_csv("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/~online/datasets/glass.data",
                  col_names = glass_col_names
                  )

Rows: 214 Columns: 11
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
dbl (11): Id, RI, Na, Mg, Al, Si, K, Ca, Ba, Fe, Type_of_glass

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

glass

10    10  1.52  13    3.6   1.36  73.0  0.57  8.4      0  0.11             1
# ℹ 204 more rows



Next we use a chain to overwrite the Type_of_glass variable and create a factor version of this variable. Then we replace the numbered factors with descriptive string factors.


glass_factors <- c(building_windows_float_processed = '1', 
                   building_windows_non_float_processed = '2',
                   vehicle_windows_float_processed = '3',
                   vehicle_windows_non_float_processed = '4',
                   containers = '5',
                   tableware = '6',
                   headlamps = '7')

glass <- glass |> 
            mutate("Type_of_glass" = as.factor(Type_of_glass)) |>
            mutate("Type_of_glass" = fct_recode(Type_of_glass,
                                                !!!glass_factors))

Warning: There was 1 warning in `mutate()`.
ℹ In argument: `Type_of_glass = fct_recode(...)`.
Caused by warning:
! Unknown levels in `f`: 4

glass

Note that while a warning is given regarding the factor level 4, viz., vehicle_windows_non_float_processed, this is merely because this factor, as indicated in the file description, has no observations in this database.

Finally, we purge the database of all observations except those where the iron, Fe, is less than \(0.2\) and the Type_of_glass is either tableware or headlamps.


glass <- glass |>
            filter((Fe < 0.3) & 
                      (Type_of_glass %in% c("tableware", "headlamps")))

glass

title: Homework 3
text:
Yeast Data
This data set, found here, is delimited by double-spaces,  a tab, evidently white spaces, but some variables have spaces within them, so we must use the read_table function, which separates columns across any whitespace.

First we read in the data, manually assigned column names.


yeast_columns <- c("seq_name", "mcg", "gvh", "alm", "mit", "erl", "pox", "vac",
                   "nuc", "class")

yeast <- read_table("https://www4.stat.ncsu.edu/online/datasets/yeast.data",
                    col_names = yeast_columns)


── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
cols(
  seq_name = col_character(),
  mcg = col_double(),
  gvh = col_double(),
  alm = col_double(),
  mit = col_double(),
  erl = col_double(),
  pox = col_double(),
  vac = col_double(),
  nuc = col_double(),
  class = col_character()
)

yeast

# A tibble: 1,484 × 10
   seq_name     mcg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac   nuc class
   <chr>      <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>
 1 ADT1_YEAST  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48  0.22 MIT  
 2 ADT2_YEAST  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53  0.22 MIT

 9 ABF2_YEAST  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49  0.22 MIT  
10 ABP1_YEAST  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58  0.3  CYT  
# ℹ 1,474 more rows



Next we remove the seq_name and nuc columns.


yeast <- yeast |>
            select(-seq_name, -nuc)

yeast

# A tibble: 1,484 × 8
     mcg   gvh   alm   mit   erl   pox   vac class
   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <chr>
 1  0.58  0.61  0.47  0.13   0.5   0    0.48 MIT  
 2  0.43  0.67  0.48  0.27   0.5   0    0.53 MIT  
 3  0.64  0.62  0.49  0.15   0.5   0    0.53 MIT  
 4  0.58  0.44  0.57  0.13   0.5   0    0.54 NUC  
 5  0.42  0.44  0.48  0.54   0.5   0    0.48 MIT  
 6  0.51  0.4   0.56  0.17   0.5   0.5  0.49 CYT  
 7  0.5   0.54  0.48  0.65   0.5   0    0.53 MIT  
 8  0.48  0.45  0.59  0.2    0.5   0    0.58 NUC  
 9  0.55  0.5   0.66  0.36   0.5   0    0.49 MIT  
10  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58 CYT  
# ℹ 1,474 more rows



Finally we add columns corresponding to the mean and median of the numeric variables, mcg, gvh, alm, mit, erl, and pox at each class grouping.


yeast <- yeast |>
            group_by(class) |>
            mutate(across(mcg:vac,
                          ~ mean(.x, na.rm = TRUE), # this part was tricky, .x is a filler for the generic row
                          .names = "mean_{.col}")) |>
            mutate(across(mcg:vac,
                          ~ median(.x, na.rm = TRUE),
                          .names = "med_{.col}"))

yeast

10  0.4   0.39  0.6   0.15   0.5   0    0.58 CYT      0.481    0.470    0.535
# ℹ 1,474 more rows
# ℹ 11 more variables: mean_mit <dbl>, mean_erl <dbl>, mean_pox <dbl>,
#   mean_vac <dbl>, med_mcg <dbl>, med_gvh <dbl>, med_alm <dbl>, med_mit <dbl>,
#   med_erl <dbl>, med_pox <dbl>, med_vac <dbl>


NB: The above was more complicated than it needed to be, accounting for NA values which did not exist in the original data set. They were introduced by incorrectly reading in the file with read_delim and using a double-space, , as the delimiter. Some of the observations had a space in the seq_name, causing the mcg column to begin with a space and thus interpreted as an NA.
